data.title: Live updates: Iranian leader vows Strait of Hormuz will stay closed; US stresses 'short-term' oil price spike
data.description:
In his first statement, Iran’s new supreme leader said Thursday morning, “The Strait of Hormuz must remain closed. American bases in the Middle East must be closed. Iran’s other military and regional capabilities will be activated if necessary.”

The Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei’s statement was read on Iranian state media.

Oil prices seesawed overnight on fears that Iran will attack ships in the Strait of Hormuz, even after an agreement Wednesday among nations to release a significant amount of petroleum reserves.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright on Thursday morning told CNN high oil prices were a “short-term energy disruption for just huge long-term gain.” An hour later, President Trump said, “when oil prices go up, we make a lot of money. BUT, of far greater interest and importance to me, as President, is stoping an evil Empire, Iran, from having Nuclear Weapons, and destroying the Middle East and, indeed, the World.”

Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, was trading at $99 around 9:45 a.m. EDT Thursday, a day after topping $100 for a second time since the military operation began 13 days ago.

Republican lawmakers are beginning to sound the alarm over the upcoming midterm elections, as rising gas prices and a slowing economy spark worries about backlash at the polls.

The Senate will again tackle Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding at nearly the one-month mark of a shutdown. Sen. Katie Britt (R-Ala.) on Wednesday blocked a Democratic effort to fund agencies within DHS that do not conduct immigration enforcement.

Trump will hold a Women’s History Month event alongside first lady Melania Trump.

Follow live below.

data.title: Bolton: US should seek out defectors in Iran in regime change push
data.description:
Former national security adviser John Bolton urged the U.S. on Wednesday to seek out defectors in Iran as President Trump continues to push for regime change nearly two weeks into the conflict.

“They need to look for defectors. People who, at this very fractured time at the top of the regime, are willing to consider coming over. And others…[who] are looking at things and saying, ‘this ship is going to go down, and I don’t want to go down with it.’ That needs to happen,” Bolton told NewsNation’s Leland Vittert on “On Balance.”

“They should be actively recruiting people in the regular military, even in the Revolutionary Guard. It’s worth a shot. And I bet there are a couple of Ayatollahs they could get as well,” he later added.

Identifying those individuals could create an opening for Iranian protesters to take to the streets in areas of the country where militias and local leaders are perceived to be less loyal to the Ayatollah, he argued.

Trump has been openly encouraging Iranian citizens to topple the government since early January, when former Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s regime began brutally cracking down on demonstrations across the country.  The president told protestors, “help is on its way,” threatening to take military action if the regime continued its lethal repression.

He renewed that call after the U.S. and Israel launched joint strikes against Tehran on Feb. 28, targeting military, nuclear and leadership sites.

“Bombs will be dropping everywhere. When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take. This will be, probably, your only chance for generations. For many years, you have asked for America’s help, but you never got it,” he said in a video message.

Fears of renewed suppression are growing inside Iran following warnings from state officials that critics in the country and abroad could face consequences for opposing the regime, according to Iran International.

The news channel reported that Iran’s police chief, Ahmadreza Radan, said in a televised interview this week that individuals who take to the streets “at the enemy’s behest” would be met with force.

“If someone comes to the streets at the enemy’s behest, we do not see them as a protester — we see them as an enemy, and we will deal with them accordingly,” Radan said. “All our forces have their fingers on the trigger and are ready.”

Bolton said Wednesday he believes Iranians are “plenty intimidated” by the regime but should not be underestimated. He also suggested that the U.S. help arm opposition forces, calling for a coordinated approach to potential uprisings.

“That’s not chump change,” he said, referring to the tens of thousands of protestors reportedly killed in recent months. “And in many parts of the country, the opposition doesn’t have weapons. That’s one thing the United States could supply them.”

“Ultimately, the Iranians are going to have to take this into their own hands. But I don’t think throwing yourself out just to get killed accomplishes anything. It needs strategy. It needs leadership. This is what America can work with — work with them on. I hope they’re doing it,” he continued.

As Iran’s new supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, continues to project defiance, Bolton acknowledged the effort could still take some time.

“We have a fantastic military, but even they can’t bring down a regime that’s governed with an iron fist for 47 years in two weeks,” he said.

data.title: Pelosi backing Dorothy McAuliffe congressional bid in Virginia
data.description:
Former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) endorsed former Virginia First Lady Dorothy McAuliffe’s run for Congress in the state’s 7th Congressional District. 

McAuliffe, the wife of former Virginia Gov. Terry McAuliffe (D) and a State Department official during the Biden administration, launched her campaign on Wednesday.

”Dorothy McAuliffe has spent her life fighting for children and families and standing up for people who need someone in their corner,” Pelosi said in a release from Dorothy McAuliffe’s campaign. “From tackling childhood hunger to supporting military families and helping survivors of sexual violence find justice. Dorothy has never backed down from the fights that matter. I’m proud to endorse her for Congress.”

Dorothy McAuliffe said she was “deeply honored” to have the support of Pelosi, a two-time Speaker who is retiring from Congress in January. 

“We have worked together for years on issues affecting children and families, and I’m grateful for her support as we build a campaign focused on lowering costs, protecting our children, and standing up for Virginia families,” the Democratic candidate added.

In addition to Pelosi, Virginia state Sen. President Pro Tempore L. Louise Lucas (D), former state Attorney General Mary Sue Terry and former Speaker of the Virginia House of Delegates Eileen Filler-Corn (D) have endorsed Dorothy McAuliffe, according to her campaign. 

The current 7th district, which encompasses parts of Northern and Cenrtral Virginia, is represented by Democratic Rep. Eugene Vindman. But voters in the Old Dominion will decide on April 21 whether to adopt a constitutional amendment to allow a new Democratic-drawn congressional map to take effect, bypassing the state’s redistricting commission. 

Under the proposed map, the 7th district would be redrawn to include parts of Fairfax, Arlington and Rockingham counties, along with all of Culpeper, Louisa and Orange counties, among others in the region. 

The new map, if implemented, would give Democrats the opportunity to boost their edge in the state from 6-5 to 10-1. 

The primary will be hold on Aug. 4.

data.title: Anti-war activists in Congress flex limited procedural muscles
data.description:
It is sometimes tempting to read the procedural tea leaves of Congress for some deeper meanings, interpretations or even occasional partisan power ploys.

In reality, most procedures observed in Congress are relatively routine and boilerplate phrases dictated by written rules, precedents and practices of each house. It is those slightly offbeat instances when something seems extraordinary that you dive deeper into what it is they are trying to do, and why. Usually these anomalous usages are initiated by the minority party which otherwise has no control over scheduling or the procedures by which measures are considered.

The issue of congressional and presidential war powers is one over which constitutional scholars and political practitioners have been wrangling since the birth of the Constitution. While it seems simple enough on its face that the Congress has the power to declare war and the president has the power as commander in chief to prosecute a war once the military has been “called into the actual service of the United States,” the Framers left it ambiguous as to the relationship of those two roles, especially after Congress has authorized a standing military.

With the initiation of the U.S. war on Iran on Feb. 28, all of the questions of the legitimacy of the attack came into play once again. The War Powers Resolution of 1973 was an attempt to clarify the blurred role of the two branches, especially since the era of declared wars ended with World War II.

As might be expected, though, the tug and pull between the branches only intensified. President Richard Nixon vetoed the war powers resolution as unconstitutional, and the Congress responded by overriding his veto. Every president since has supported Nixon’s contention that the act is unconstitutional because it interferes with the president’s prerogatives as commander in chief.

Most, however, have abided by its timely reporting requirements, including for an initial statement on the justification for the military action. President Trump has complied with that, both in the initial Iran bombing strike and the latest takeout of the Iranian leader and high command. Under the terms of the War Powers Resolution, the president now has 60 days to terminate the military hostilities, unless Congress enacts a 30-day extension.

What Democrats in Congress have attempted, both in last June’s strike against Iran’s nuclear facilities and again in the Feb. 28 attack on Iran, is to terminate the American military involvement immediately by passing a bipartisan concurrent resolution. There weren’t enough votes to discharge the Senate measure, which invoked the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act, from the Foreign Relations Committee. The House measure never received a vote.

This year, on March 4 and 5, the Senate rejected an identical Senate motion to discharge a resolution from the Foreign Relations Committee by an identical vote of 47 to 53, with only one member of each party breaking ranks. Sen. Tim Kaine (D-Va.) did not use any of the hour of debate time allotted him under the law.

In the House, the concurrent resolution sponsored by Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), invoking the War Powers Act, was rejected outright, 212 to 219. Two Republicans voted for it and four Democrats voted against.

So, what are we to make of this? First, members of Congress are reluctant to second-guess their president at the outset of a war and order the immediate cessation of hostilities. Second, members of the president’s party are more willing to give their support than are opposition party members. Third, the procedural means being used relying on differing statutory justifications are irrelevant. The president will oppose whatever Congress does to stop him, and, in the case of the Senate joint resolution, will veto it if it ever reaches his desk.

Fourth, as the war drags on, these positions in Congress may vary somewhat, but especially if the president puts boots on the ground to effect regime change.

Finally, the only real power the Congress has over the president in such military ventures is in exercising its power of the purse. If the president comes to Congress for supplemental funding for the war, that is where Congress will most likely flex its fiscal muscles. Forever wars were not originally on the Trump agenda, and he has a party elephant base that never forgets.

Don Wolfensberger is a 28-year congressional staff veteran, culminating as chief of staff of the House Rules Committee in 1995. He is author of, “Congress and the People: Deliberative Democracy on Trial” (2000), and, “Changing Cultures in Congress: From Fair Play to Power Plays” (2018).

data.title: Fetterman on Iran's new supreme leader: 'They should kill him, too'
data.description:
Sen. John Fetterman (D-Pa.) on Wednesday said Israel should kill Iran’s newly appointed supreme leader.

Mojtaba Khamenei was named to lead Iran on Monday after his father, Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed in U.S.-Israeli airstrikes.

“Well, they should kill him, too…hopefully he is significantly wounded. And if he does recover, you know, I do absolutely support having Israel to just eliminate him, you know, along with any other parts of the leadership,” Fetterman said during an appearance on NewsNation’s “Elizabeth Vargas Tonight.” 

“I mean, I’m always going to support that.  I think it’s a good thing to continue to eliminate leaders of Hezbollah, Hamas and the Iranian, too,” he added.

President Trump said he was “not happy” with the selection of Khamenei as the successor for the Iranian regime.

Fetterman has been one of the few Democrats who has lauded Trump’s strikes on Tehran in Operation Epic Fury. On Wednesday, the Pennsylvania senator said the series of offensive assaults have been a “success thus far” in breaking and destroying the Iranian nuclear apparatus.

Fetterman notably did not sign on to a letter urging the Trump administration to address a strike on an Iranian school for girls that a preliminary investigation found the U.S. was likely responsible for.

He told Vargas, “my red line is boots on the ground.”

Fetterman later questioned apprehension from his fellow party members.

“I would just like to remind everybody watching that every single Democratic presidential candidate always identified that Iran is a top, top security issue. And every single Democrat in the Senate and in the House, you know, says we can never allow Iran to acquire a nuclear bomb. And Kamala Harris, when she was running just back in ’24, she identified Iran as her top, top international concern,” Fetterman said. 

“And now she, of course, would never allow Iran to acquire a nuclear bomb. And now someone actually made that virtually impossible after this operation. I truly don’t understand why Democrats can’t even acknowledge that this has made the world more safe and more secure, you know, not just all of us, but not just especially the region as well, too.”

data.title: Clyburn says he'll seek an 18th term in South Carolina
data.description:
Rep. Jim Clyburn (D-S.C.), a Democratic titan of the House, announced Thursday that he will seek reelection this year for an 18th term, ending the speculation that he was ready to retire at the end of this Congress. 

Speaking to reporters in South Carolina, Clyburn, 85, said he will file the paperwork to run again in November’s midterms to keep his seat in the sprawling 6th District that Clyburn has held since 1993. 

“I have served in public life all of my life,” Clyburn said from the South Carolina Democratic Party headquarters in Columbia.

Clyburn’s decision means that he will not join Reps. Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) and Steny Hoyer (D-Md.), two other former Democratic leaders who had both announced earlier in the term that they will retire at the end of this Congress. The move all but ensures Clyburn’s return to Capitol Hill for another two years.

But the decision is sure to rankle some in the party amid an internal battle over generational change and a push to elevate younger talent on Capitol Hill.

Clyburn had served with Pelosi and Hoyer as the top House Democrats for nearly two decades, beginning in 2006, and helped to usher in some of the most far-reaching legislation of the last half-century, including ObamaCare and Congress’s response to the COVID-19 pandemic. 

But perhaps Clyburn’s greatest influence over the party in recent years has related to his role as a kingmaker in his home state of South Carolina, where his enthusiastic support for Joe Biden was a tipping point in Biden’s successful bid for the Democratic nomination — and ultimately the White House — in 2020.

Heading into the state’s primary in February of that year, Biden’s top opponent, Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), had all the momentum, having won a landslide in Nevada the previous weekend. And there was plenty of speculation that if Biden lost the Palmetto State, his run at the White House was essentially over.

Clyburn, a well-known powerbroker in the state, jumped into the race at the last moment, endorsing Biden three days before the contest. That set the stage for Biden’s lopsided victory — he beat Sanders by almost 30 points — and his eventual nomination at the Democratic convention later in the year.

First elected in 1992, Clyburn quickly made a mark on Capitol Hill, serving as head of the Congressional Black Caucus before being elected as vice chair of the full Democratic Caucus in 2003. 

From there, he rose in 2006 to chair the Democratic Caucus, and in 2007 rose again to Democratic whip, the third-ranking spot, where he served just below Pelosi and Hoyer until 2023, when Republicans seized control of the House and the top three Democratic leaders all stepped down from their spots. 

While Pelosi and Hoyer resigned from leadership altogether, however, Clyburn decided to remain, winning the position of assistant Democratic leader in the last Congress. The move infuriated some younger members of the caucus, who wanted to clear the field for generational change across the leadership ranks.

Clyburn stepped down from that leadership spot in early 2024, replaced by Rep. Joe Neguse (D-Colo.).

data.title: UN agency says up to 3.2 million displaced by Iran war
data.description:
Millions of Iranians have been at least temporarily displaced amid the joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on the country, according to a new estimate from the United Nations Refugee Agency (UNHCR). 

The UNHCR said Thursday that 3.2 million people in the country have been forced to leave their homes because of the ongoing military operations. This number represents up to 1 million households, according to the agency. 

Ayako Ito, who is directing the agency’s response to this conflict, said in Thursday’s press release that the total is “likely to continue rising as hostilities persist, marking a worrying escalation in humanitarian needs.”

The UNHCR coordinates the international responses to refugee crises and provides essential services, including shelter, food and medical care, to people fleeing conflict and persecution. 

“Also affected are refugee families hosted in the country, mostly Afghans, who are particularly vulnerable, given their already precarious situation and limited support networks,” Ito continued. “Families are leaving affected areas amid rising insecurity and limited access to essential services.”

Ito said the agency is collaborating with national authorities and partners to meet the rising need for its services in Iran. 

“UNCHR emphasizes the urgent need to protect civilians, maintain humanitarian access, and ensure borders remain open to those seeking safety, in line with international obligations,” Ito wrote in the press release. 

The U.S. and Israel launched joint attacks on Iran on Feb. 28, setting off a chain of military operations between the countries in the Middle East. 

After missile strikes killed Iranian Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and bombarded Tehran, the Iranians responded with drone strikes on U.S. bases in the region, killing seven U.S. servicemembers.

The State Department said on Wednesday that more than 43,000 U.S. citizens have safely returned from the Middle East since Feb. 28 as Iran continues to launch strikes on the Gulf Nations. The department has offered government-chartered flights to assist Americans wishing to leave the region. 

State Department Assistant Secretary Dylan Johnson said that the department has directly helped 30,000 Americans through a newly-created 24/7 task force. 

Despite these efforts, Johnson said, “Most Americans who requested assistance have declined assistance when offered, opting either to remain in country or book commercial flight options.”

data.title: Retired general: Trump would be 'smart' to 'declare victory' in Iran and 'go home'
data.description:
Retired Army Brig. Gen. Steve Anderson said Wednesday that President Trump would be “smart” to “declare victory” in the conflict against Iran and “go home.”

“Well, this war will end whenever Donald Trump says it’s going to end, whenever he decides to declare victory. And if he was smart, he’d declare victory today and go home, because this is not going well. We have a more radicalized regime in place now,” Anderson told CNN’s Wolf Blitzer on “The Situation Room.”

“The [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps] does not seem to be impacted at all. In fact, they’ve become perhaps even stronger. Sure, they’ve lost a lot of military facilities, they’ve lost a lot of assets, but there’s nothing that they can’t reconstitute quickly,” he added.

On Tuesday, officials from the Pentagon told lawmakers during a briefing that the approximate cost of war against Iran was more than $11.3 billion in the fighting’s first six days, a source told The Hill.

Shipping traffic has been held up in the Strait of Hormuz for close to two weeks during the conflict against Iran, but Trump said Wednesday the strait was in “great shape” in spite of rising concern over the effect of a blockade that lasts.

“We’ve knocked out all of their boats,” Trump told reporters at Joint Base Andrews. “They have some missiles, but not very many. I think we’re in very good…we’re in very good shape.”

Close to a fifth of the world’s oil supply goes through the Strait of Hormuz, but the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) has said it won’t let “even a single liter of oil” go through the strait until strikes from the U.S. and Israel stop.

Iran’s new supreme leader, the Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, said in a Thursday morning statement that the strait “must remain closed.”

“American bases in the Middle East must be closed. Iran’s other military and regional capabilities will be activated if necessary,” the statement continued.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: A legalization program even Republicans can support
data.description:
At a meeting with a bipartisan group of congressmen during his first term in office, President Trump explained that he wanted to provide lawful status for participants in the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals program, and that this was just the first phase of a plan for overhauling our immigration laws. 

Let’s do a legalization program for them first, he said, then work on a comprehensive immigration reform bill. Trump’s plan was abandoned when negotiations ended without an agreement.  

Circumstances have changed since then. The border is now secure. Interior enforcement of our immigration laws has been so successful that 1.9 million undocumented immigrants have left the U.S. voluntarily since January 2025.   

Today, the Republicans need to show that their immigration enforcement measures are not motivated by animosity toward immigrants. A recent YouGov poll indicates that opposition to Republican immigration measures is increasing. Support for abolishing ICE has reached 50 percent, and that number includes 52 percent of independents.   

A bill to update the registry provision in the Immigration and Nationality Act would be a good first step — and it could have enough bipartisan support to be enacted if it is amended to restrict the lawful status it provides. 

The Registry Act of 1929 established a process for immigrants to create a record of lawful admission for permanent residence. They were required to establish that they had entered the U.S. before 1921, had a residence in the United States continuously since that entry, and could meet the other eligibility requirements. Congress has updated the registry date eight times since 1929; the last update was made in 1986 by the Immigration Reform and Control Act, changing the registry date to 1972.

The failure to update it again in the 40 years since means that registry eligibility now requires continuous residence for more than 54 years. An applicant must also establish that he or she is not deportable under specified provisions, is a person of good moral character, is not ineligible for citizenship, and that relief is merited as a matter of discretion. 

What we ought to do now is amend the registry provision to make it the basis for a revocable lawful status with fewer benefits than the lawful permanent resident status that the registry provision currently provides. It could be revoked if the recipient of this status violates specified laws or meets other conditions subject to negotiation. If it is revoked, the alien’s immigration status would revert back to what it was before he or she obtained revocable lawful status.

Revocable status would just permit some otherwise deportable aliens to live and work in the U.S. lawfully. It would limit derivative status to spouses and children. Moreover, eligibility would be limited to aliens with no convictions for criminal offenses outside of illegal entry into the U.S. There would not be any waivers.

Lastly, there would be an annual limit on the number of aliens who could get the status that would count spouses and children. The limit would be set annually.  

Congress has not had the bipartisan support needed for a major legalization program since President Ronald Reagan signed the 1986 law. A registry provision that grants a revocable lawful status could overcome that resistance by limiting the benefits of legalization.

The long residence and clean-record requirements should make the program less likely to encourage more illegal immigration.

Updating the registry date to a more reasonable length of time would just restore functionality to an established statutory mechanism that Congress has already updated eight times.

Revocable lawful status would also not be subject to chain migration, since it limits derivative status to spouses and children.

Lastly, a common objection to legalization programs is they provide a path to citizenship. The revocable lawful status I am proposing would not provide a path to citizenship, or even to lawful permanent resident status.

I do not think it is smart for the Department of Homeland Security to use its limited resources on long-time residents with clean records. Priority should be given to undocumented aliens who pose a threat of some kind.  

If my approach is rejected, Congress should find another way to pass a limited legalization program. In addition to the benefits it would provide, Trump may still be willing to support the two-phase approach he tried to implement during his first term in office, which could lead to bipartisan support for comprehensive immigration reform.

Nolan Rappaport was detailed to the House Judiciary Committee as an Executive Branch Immigration Law Expert for three years. He subsequently served as an immigration counsel for the Subcommittee on Immigration, Border Security and Claims for four years. Prior to working on the Judiciary Committee, he wrote decisions for the Board of Immigration Appeals for 20 years.

data.title: Florida attorney general threatens Tampa mayor with removal over immigration policies
data.description:
TAMPA, Fla. (WFLA) — Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier sent a letter to Tampa Mayor Jane Castor on Wednesday saying the state will take action if she does not stop “forcing sanctuary policies” on the Tampa Police Department (TPD).

“TPD’s policies prohibiting its officers from sharing certain information with federal immigration authorities and limiting the immigration enforcement activities in which it participates effectively establishes a sanctuary policy,” Uthmeier wrote in the letter.

Uthmeier took issue with TPD withholding information from federal immigration authorities about victims and witnesses of crimes, specifically concerning their legal status. This policy aims to reassure people who are in the country illegally that they will not face possible detention and deportation for coming forward to report a crime.

“But we want illegal aliens to fear immigration consequences to the extent they are here unlawfully,” Uthmeier wrote.

Protections exist for victims of certain crimes who could face removal from the country if investigated by federal immigration officials, as required by state law. The Department of Homeland Security website, cited in the letter, highlights pathways to temporary legal immigration status for victims of human trafficking and domestic violence, as well as over two dozen other crimes.

“This is going to prevent illegal immigrants — yes, they are illegal — but people who do not have a criminal record to have access to benefits that they could have if they are able to report the crime,” said immigration attorney Lisette Sanchez.

TPD also “prohibits officers from engaging in ‘broad-based’ immigration enforcement actions,” which Uthmeier called an “ambiguous restriction on immigration enforcement activities” and said the city “must do better.” He argued the policies “jeopardize the safety of Tampa residents.”

“Given the danger illegals pose to Florida, the Mayor of Tampa and TPD should prioritize the safety of Americans over the comfort of illegal criminal aliens who have no right to be here in the first place,” Uthmeier wrote.

The attorney general ordered the city to reverse the immigration policies by March 31. Uthmeier threatened Castor with removal by the governor if the city does not comply.

During a press conference in June 2025, Castor spoke about immigration enforcement.

“Those immigrant populations become the victims of robberies and they are not comfortable reporting that. They are not comfortable reporting the fraud because they do not trust law enforcement and that is the fundamental reason that local law enforcement should not be engaged in immigration enforcement,” said Castor. “If we are looking for individuals who are committing crime in our communities, and everyone can agree that we do not want criminals in our communities, in our neighborhoods, but if you go looking for immigrants as the cause of that crime, that is a fool’s path because immigrants are coming to this country by and large to realize the American dream and everyone in this room has descended from immigrants.”

In the letter, Uthmeier said there are procedures in place to protect the integrity of a case when a victim or witness is in the United States unlawfully. The letter went on to say how immigration authorities must be alerted to individuals in the country illegally and perform investigations of their status irrespective of their role in a case.

“It is a great concern because those people that are right now the victims of crimes, they’re not going to be able to go forward and to go and create … these complaints and maybe to report the crime,” said Sanchez. “And, you know, in my opinion, there are so many crimes that not necessarily are committed by immigrants. Those immigrants can be victims or they can be witnesses of crimes committed by citizens of this country.”

Castor issued the following statement on Wednesday afternoon:

“In light of the Attorney General’s letter today, the City of Tampa will review the concerns raised and evaluate our policies and procedures to ensure that we use best efforts to support the enforcement of federal immigration law.

“Tampa is one of the safest cities of our size in the nation because we built trust with our community through collaboration. The Tampa Police Department signed the 287(g) and developed its immigration enforcement policy in consultation with partner agencies and law enforcement associations to ensure all immigration-related actions are carried out according to state and federal law.”

Castor would not be the first Hillsborough County official removed by Gov. Ron DeSantis. In 2022, State Attorney Andrew Warren was ousted from his position over what DeSantis described as leniency and refusal to enforce certain laws.
